Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Marguerite Porete

d. 1310

A systematic reconstruction from The Mirror of Simple Souls and multilingual scholarship

I use “mind” here in a careful reconstructed sense. Porete’s own central term is the “soul,” not “mind” in the modern analytic sense. But her “soul” includes what later philosophy would divide into cognition, affect, will, attention, selfhood, agency, conscience, imagination, sensation, and spiritual perception. A philosophy of mind can therefore be extracted from her work, provided that we do not reduce her theology to psychology or reduce her psychology to doctrine. In Porete, God’s knowing, willing, and loving, and the mental life of angels, are not decorative theological motifs. They are boundary cases for what intelligence, freedom, and love can be.

Marguerite Porete was a late thirteenth- and early fourteenth-century beguine associated with northern France or Hainaut, author of The Mirror of Simple Souls. Her book circulated in vernacular and Latin traditions, was condemned, and she was executed in Paris on June 1, 1310. Modern scholarship has strongly complicated the older tendency to read her simply as a “Free Spirit” antinomian; her work is now commonly read as a sophisticated intervention in mystical theology, vernacular philosophy, and late-medieval debates about will, virtue, and contemplative knowledge.

1. The central thesis: the soul’s highest mind is not self-possession but dispossession

A first formulation of Porete’s philosophy of mind would be this:

For the soul to be most fully itself is not for it to intensify its own autonomous self-consciousness, self-direction, or self-will. It is for it to cease possessing itself as its own, so that God’s knowing, loving, and willing may operate in it “without” its proprietary mediation.

This sounds paradoxical because modern accounts of mind often identify mentality with self-possession: I think, I choose, I intend, I remember, I desire. Porete’s highest soul is not mindless, but it is no longer governed by “mine-ness.” Her concern is not the annihilation of the soul as a creature, but the annihilation of “proper will,” self-enclosed agency, and the egoic claim over mental life. The result is not blankness. It is a higher mode of cognition and freedom.

The Mirror is deliberately dramatic and polyphonic: Love, Reason, the Soul, the Virtues, Courtesy, and different modes of understanding speak in conflict. French scholarship has rightly stressed that this form is not ornamental; the drama stages rival mental regimes. Reason and “the understanding of reason” confront Love and “the understanding of love,” while the Soul is gradually drawn from mediated, discursive, moralized self-management into immediate, passive, substantial contemplative knowing.

The crucial point is that Porete’s “annihilated soul” is not a destroyed subject. It is a subject whose mental ownership has been unmade. The soul no longer treats cognition, virtue, prayer, desire, or good works as possessions through which it secures itself. It is “without itself,” not because it has no being, but because its being is received, offered back, and non-proprietary. This is why Porete can say the soul “has no will” while also insisting that free will is given by God and cannot simply be taken away. The will is not erased by force; it freely returns itself to God.

2. The soul as created “nothing”: ontology before psychology

Porete’s psychology begins in metaphysics. The soul is not an isolated mental substance that first exists and then happens to relate to God. It is a created being whose reality depends wholly on God. Hence her repeated language of “naught,” “nothing,” and annihilation.

This “nothingness” should not be read as nihilism. It means that the soul has no self-grounding being. God alone is Being in the full sense; the creature is real by gift, dependence, and participation. In the fifth state of the soul, Porete says, the soul sees that God is all and that she herself is not. This recognition is not despair. It is the cognitive and affective event by which self-appropriation collapses. The soul’s own nothingness becomes liberating because it releases the soul from the fantasy of possessing itself.

This has major consequences for philosophy of mind. Mental life is not ultimately self-founded. The soul does not possess thought, desire, virtue, or even prayer as autonomous properties. The mind’s deepest truth is dependency. Its error is not merely false belief, but false ownership: treating divine gifts as “mine,” treating spiritual progress as “my achievement,” and treating God as an object to be acquired by my acts.

Porete’s soul is therefore not an ego-substance seeking religious experiences. It is a created center of cognition, will, love, and receptivity whose perfection consists in recognizing that it has no self-sufficient ground. The soul’s “nothingness” is the ontological condition for its highest form of intelligence.

3. The architecture of mind: faculties, powers, and regimes

Porete’s text presupposes a faculty psychology, but she does not merely repeat scholastic commonplaces. She dramatizes the faculties and shows how they are reconfigured by mystical transformation.

Reason: necessary, limited, and finally displaced

Reason is one of the most important characters in the Mirror. It asks questions, objects, fails to understand, demands distinctions, and often speaks from the perspective of moral and ecclesial order. Reason is not simply evil. It is pedagogically necessary. Yet Porete treats it as limited to a lower range of cognition.

The French and German receptions are especially useful here. French scholarship emphasizes the opposition between Entendement de raison and Entendement d’Amour: the “understanding of reason” remains mediated, discursive, and theological in the ordinary sense, whereas the “understanding of love” becomes immediate and passive. A German reading goes further, interpreting Reason in the Mirror as a degraded or institutionalized intellect tied to fear, ordinance, scholastic disputation, and inquisitorial discipline; yet the same reading cautions that Porete is not replacing thinking with blind faith, but exposing the poverty of a certain kind of thought and experimenting with another.

This is important. Porete is not anti-intellectual in the crude sense. She is anti-reductionist about reason. Discursive reason can teach, distinguish, test, and lead the soul toward the threshold. But it cannot cross into the highest divine knowing as master. It must lose jurisdiction.

Dubois’s study of Porete and university virtue debates helps correct simplistic readings: Reason and the Virtues are not simply discarded as worthless; rather, they are no longer sovereign once the soul has entered a higher relation to divine love. Reason remains indispensable for the inquiry that leads toward annihilation, even though it cannot govern the final state.

So Porete’s theory of reason is hierarchical and transformational. Reason is a real faculty. It is not the highest faculty. It becomes dangerous when it mistakes its range for the whole of mind.

Understanding of Love: cognition transformed by affect

Porete’s most important contribution to philosophy of mind may be her refusal to separate love from intelligence. Love is not merely an emotion added to cognition. Love is a way of knowing.

Spanish scholarship has made this especially explicit, reading Porete as presenting two forms of knowledge: the understanding of reason and the understanding of love. Love is not opposed to knowing; it is a transformed cognition, comparable to the medieval intellectus amoris, an intellect of love.

In the Mirror, Love is “Mother of the Knowing of Divine Light.” This phrase is philosophically dense. It means that the highest cognition is generated by love, not by neutral observation. The soul knows God, itself, and all things differently when it is loved into divine light. Love changes the subject’s mode of access. It does not merely provide motivation; it restructures cognition.

For modern philosophy of mind, this is a robust affective-cognitive model. Porete denies that cognition is best understood as detached representation. Some truths are inaccessible unless the knower’s affective orientation has been transformed. Love is not bias; rightly ordered love is the condition of higher objectivity.

Will: the decisive faculty

The will is the central battleground of Porete’s psychology. The soul’s bondage lies in “proper will,” that is, will as self-owning, self-directed, and self-securing. The soul’s freedom lies in “not-willing,” not as passivity or incapacity, but as the relinquishment of private voluntarism into divine will.

Peter King’s philosophical reconstruction is especially helpful here. He reads Porete in relation to Godfrey of Fontaines and highlights three striking theses: the will is only contingently connected to the intellect and can be “detached”; moral virtues are neither necessary nor sufficient for the highest state; and love literally transforms the self.

Porete herself insists on a paradox: the soul should will the divine will “without willing.” In ordinary terms, this seems contradictory. In her framework, it means that the soul no longer wills as proprietor. The will does not vanish; it ceases to stand apart as an appropriating faculty. It becomes identical in orientation with divine will while remaining creaturely.

This is not determinism. Porete’s own treatment of sin and consent shows that will remains morally decisive. No sin occurs without consent of the will; bodily inclination alone does not constitute sin. God gives free will, and no power can take it away unless the will itself yields. Thus the highest “no-will” is not coercion by God but the free self-offering of freedom back to its source.

Memory, understanding, and will: a Trinitarian psychology

Porete also offers a trinitarian mapping of the soul’s powers. In one crucial passage, memory is related to the Father, understanding to the Son’s wisdom, and will to the Holy Spirit’s goodness. The divine Trinity is not external to her philosophy of mind; it is the archetype by which the human soul’s powers are interpreted.

This means that Porete’s “mind” cannot be reconstructed adequately as a secular faculty system with theology stripped away. Her account of memory, understanding, and will is inseparable from participation in Father, Son, and Spirit. Human mental life images divine life, but only becomes fully itself when it is reabsorbed into the divine order through love.

This also prevents a purely individualistic reading. The soul is not a private interior chamber. Its faculties are relationally structured from the beginning. Memory, understanding, and will are not merely internal mechanisms; they are creaturely analogues of divine substance, wisdom, and love.

The senses and the body: not destroyed, but decentered

Porete’s highest soul does not become a disembodied angel while still on earth. She repeatedly qualifies the soul’s freedom in this life. The soul is not permanently free in the absolute sense while embodied; “sensuality” and bodily instability remain.

At the same time, the highest soul can lose the “usage” of the wits or senses without losing the senses themselves. This is an important distinction. Porete does not describe the destruction of embodied faculties. She describes a suspension or transformation of their ordinary use. Love ravishes the soul away from former sensory and cognitive habits; the faculties remain, but their governance changes.

This gives Porete a subtle embodied psychology. The body remains a site of inclination, instability, and limitation, but not necessarily of corruption. The decisive moral factor is consent. Bodily movement without consent is not sin. This allows her to distinguish between involuntary psychophysical life and the will’s appropriation of that life.

4. The seven states as a developmental philosophy of consciousness

Porete’s seven states of the soul can be read as a phenomenology of spiritual-mental development. They describe not merely moral progress but transformations in cognition, desire, agency, attention, and self-relation.

In the first state, the soul lives according to commandments. Its mind is governed by obedience, fear, and basic moral order. This is not despised; it is the necessary beginning.

In the second state, the soul moves beyond commandments toward counsel and imitation. It begins to mortify nature and orient itself toward a more demanding perfection.

In the third state, the soul practices works of perfection with love, sacrificing its own will through disciplined obedience and moral exertion. This is still an active, effortful, virtue-centered mode of mind.

The fourth state is contemplative delight. The soul is ravished by sweetness in meditation and contemplation. Yet Porete treats this state as spiritually dangerous because sweetness can deceive. The soul may become attached to contemplative pleasure and mistake affective exaltation for final union.

This is a psychologically acute point. Porete recognizes a form of spiritual narcissism: the soul may love its own loving, delight in its own devotion, or mistake tenderness for God. Elsewhere she warns that the tenderness of love may secretly be self-love “without her witting.” Her theory of mind therefore includes a critique of introspection. The soul is not always transparent to itself; it can misread its own affective states.

The fifth state is decisive. The soul sees that God is all and that she herself is nothing. Free will returns to God. The soul is “annihilated” in the sense that it no longer possesses proper will. This is the birth of “free being.”

The sixth state is the highest state available intermittently in this life. Here God sees in the soul, and the soul sees nothing but God. The soul does not know, love, or praise by ordinary creaturely effort; God’s own operation becomes primary. Yet this state is not permanent in embodied life. Porete speaks of swift opening and closing, a temporary ravishment.

The seventh state is reserved for glory after death, when the soul leaves the body. This confirms that Porete is not claiming that embodied humans can permanently become indistinguishable from glorified divine life in the present world. Her most radical language is bounded by an eschatological reserve.

The seven states therefore form a philosophy of consciousness in which the mind moves from law-governed agency, to virtue-governed agency, to contemplative affect, to annihilated selfhood, to intermittent divine seeing, and finally to post-mortem glory.

5. Selfhood: from “with herself” to “without herself”

One of Porete’s most powerful distinctions is between the soul being “with herself” and “without herself.” To be “with herself” is not simply to be conscious. It is to be self-appropriating: to will, desire, act, pray, know, or love as one’s own possession.

When the soul wills anything in this proprietary way, Porete says, it is “with itself” and has lost freedom. True freedom is “free being,” a condition in which the soul is no longer governed by self-reference.

This does not mean that the self is metaphysically eliminated. Porete often preserves a distinction of natures: one being, one will, one love, and one work “in two natures.” The phrase is crucial. The highest union is not a crude fusion in which the creature simply becomes God by essence. Rather, the creature participates so completely in divine operation that the action is one, while the difference of divine and created natures remains.

Her selfhood model is therefore neither modern individualism nor simple pantheistic absorption. It is participatory dispossession. The self is most free when it no longer treats itself as the origin, owner, or beneficiary of its own mental acts.

A useful modern analogy, used cautiously, would be “ego dissolution,” but Porete’s account is more normatively and metaphysically specific. The goal is not merely diminished self-referential processing. It is union with divine will through charity and annihilation of proper will.

6. Epistemology: knowing by unknowing

Porete’s theory of cognition is apophatic: the soul comes to know by knowing that it does not know. But this unknowing is not ignorance. It is a superior cognition that recognizes the inadequacy of ordinary mental grasp.

In the “far night,” which Porete associates with the Trinity, divine knowing gives the soul knowledge of God, self, and all things in God’s being, but also takes away knowledge of God, self, and all things. This paradox is the heart of her epistemology. The soul knows by being drawn beyond objectifying knowledge.

Spanish and Latin American discussions capture this well: Porete’s soul enters a knowledge in unknowing, a “more” beyond human understanding, where mystical excess passes beyond ordinary thought and into love.

This has several philosophical implications.

First, Porete rejects the idea that the highest knowledge is representation. God is not known as an object placed before the mind. God is known through participation, transformation, and divine self-communication.

Second, she rejects the idea that self-knowledge is transparent introspection. The soul truly knows itself only by seeing its nothingness before God. It does not gain a richer empirical profile of itself; it loses the illusion of self-sufficiency.

Third, she treats humility as epistemic, not merely moral. To humble one’s “science” is to let love and faith exceed reason. The French textual tradition explicitly says that theologians and clerics will not understand the book unless they proceed humbly and unless love and faith surpass reason.

Fourth, she distinguishes between lower mediated knowing and higher immediate knowing. The soul no longer seeks God “here or there” as an object among objects. It does not seek divine science merely from earthly masters. In the highest state, it receives “without intermediary.”

This does not mean all mediation is evil. It means that mediation is not the final form of divine cognition.

7. Divine mind: God’s knowing, willing, and loving as the archetype of mind

A rigorous extraction of Porete’s philosophy of mind must treat the divine mind as central. Porete does not present God as a “mind” in the modern psychological sense. God is not an enlarged human subject with mental states. But divine knowing, willing, loving, wisdom, goodness, and vision are the archetypes from which human mental life receives its structure and into which it is drawn.

God’s will gives being to free creatures. God’s knowing gives the annihilated soul knowledge and then removes objectifying knowledge. God’s love transforms the soul. God’s seeing becomes the soul’s seeing in the sixth state. This is not a metaphorical supplement to her psychology; it is the core mechanism of higher consciousness.

The divine mind in Porete is trinitarian. The Father is associated with substance, the Son with wisdom or fruition, and the Holy Spirit with conjunction and goodness. The soul’s memory, understanding, and will are mapped onto this triune divine structure.

This produces a strong thesis: human mind is not autonomous cognition aiming at God as an external object; human mind is a created participation in divine life. At the summit, God does divine works “in us, for us, without us.” That “without us” does not mean against us, but without proprietary human authorship.

Porete’s account of paradise also matters. Paradise is not primarily a place or reward external to cognition; paradise is God himself, and seeing God is paradise. Beatitude is therefore an epistemic-affective state: vision, love, and participation in divine being.

So, if we reconstruct a Poretean account of divine mind, it has these features: it is simple rather than discursive, creative rather than merely representational, loving rather than affectively neutral, and participatory rather than external. The perfected human mind does not mirror God from outside; it is seen by God and sees by God.

8. Angelic minds: Seraphim, guardian angels, and Lucifer

Porete’s angelology is philosophically significant. Angels supply models of created mentality without the same bodily encumbrance as humans. They show both the height of created love and the danger of misdirected will.

The Seraphim are especially important. Porete compares the free soul to a being with six wings like the Seraphim, and she says that there is no intermediary between seraphic love and divine love. The annihilated soul receives “without mean,” that is, without intermediary, in an analogous way.

This gives us a model of immediate created cognition/love. Seraphic mind is not primarily discursive. It is ordered by direct love. The free soul does not literally become an angel, but seraphic immediacy helps Porete describe the soul’s highest possible mode in this life.

Guardian angels supply another model: action without encumbrance. Porete says the soul is no more burdened by outward works through the senses than a guardian angel is burdened by guarding the soul. This is a striking theory of non-possessive agency. Action can occur without the agent being inwardly weighed down by ownership of the act.

Lucifer and the fallen angels supply the negative case. Porete interprets Lucifer’s fall as a perverse election of will: he desired by nature what could only be received by grace, and his will caused the loss of high vision.

This is central to her philosophy of mind. The highest created intelligence can fall if will seeks possession of what must be received as gift. The angelic fall is therefore not a marginal mythological episode. It clarifies the danger at the heart of mind: intelligence without humble receptivity becomes appropriation.

Angelic minds also allow Porete to distinguish hierarchy without reducing all creatures to one level. All angels are angels, but not all are Seraphim; analogously, “soul” and “spirit” can name different degrees or modes of spiritual mentality.

9. Moral psychology: virtue, law, sin, and freedom

Porete’s most controversial claims concern the soul’s relation to virtues, works, and law. The annihilated soul “takes leave” of the Virtues and is “above law.” Read crudely, this sounds antinomian. Read carefully, it is a theory of moral transformation.

The soul at lower stages needs virtues as governors. The Virtues teach, discipline, restrain, and form the soul. But at the highest stage, the soul no longer relates to virtues as external mistresses. The virtues are not necessarily absent; rather, the soul is not under them as under a regime of effortful self-management.

Recent French scholarship on the “superfluity” of virtues stresses that Porete’s annihilation is the state in which the soul is divested of all proper will; before that, the soul performs works of virtue, but these can become burdens. The important distinction is between possessing virtues and performing the “works of virtues” as self-directed efforts.

Dubois similarly argues that Porete’s treatment of the virtues should be placed in thirteenth-century university debates. On this reading, the abandonment of virtues is not a simple rejection of moral life but an affirmation that only supernatural virtues lead the soul to God.

Porete’s own formula “above law, not against law” is decisive. The annihilated soul is not licensed to violate divine order. Rather, it is no longer governed by law as an external constraint because divine love has become inwardly operative.

Her account of sin confirms this. Sin requires consent of will. Bodily impulses, involuntary movements, and creaturely instability do not constitute sin unless the will consents. That is a sophisticated moral psychology: Porete distinguishes sensation, inclination, consent, and moral authorship.

The result is neither legalism nor lawlessness. It is a hierarchy of moral agency. At lower levels, law and virtue govern the soul from outside and train it. At higher levels, the soul is governed from within by divine love, so the form of moral life changes from obedience-as-management to freedom-as-union.

10. Attention, intention, and action

Porete has a strong account of attention. Lower mental life is multiple: many intentions, many works, many fears, many devotions, many acts of self-assessment. The higher soul has “one intention.” The contrast between Mary and Martha is used to distinguish multiplicity of works from unified contemplative attention.

The annihilated soul is not inactive, but action is no longer structured by anxious self-reference. God acts in the soul, for the soul, and without the soul’s proprietary mediation. The soul’s action becomes “without why,” without self-seeking motive.

This is a philosophy of agency as non-appropriative action. The highest agent is not the one who maximizes explicit control over each act. It is the one whose will has become so aligned with divine will that action proceeds without inner division, calculation, or self-claim.

This also explains Porete’s suspicion of spiritual busyness. Prayer, fasting, sermons, works, and devotions can be good at proper stages, but they can also become ways for the soul to remain “with itself.” Reason is scandalized when Love says the free soul has no desire for sermons, fasts, or prayers. The point is not that these practices are evil; the point is that desire for them can remain self-appropriating.

11. The mind’s greatest danger: spiritual self-love

Porete is not naïve about interiority. She knows that the soul may misinterpret its own states. The fourth state, rich in contemplative sweetness, is especially dangerous because the soul may mistake spiritual pleasure for divine union.

Her later warning about the “martyrdom” of will and love deepens this. The soul may experience tenderness, devotion, or love, yet this may still be self-love without the soul knowing it.

This is one of Porete’s most philosophically modern insights. Introspection is fallible. A person may sincerely believe herself to be loving God while actually loving her own affective state, her own holiness, her own love, or her own contemplative identity.

Thus the annihilation of self is not a decorative mystical excess. It is an epistemic safeguard. Only when the soul loses ownership of its own love can it be freed from the subtle narcissism of loving itself under religious descriptions.

12. Multilingual reception: what non-Anglophone readings add

The Anglophone reception has often emphasized heresy, gender, beguine spirituality, vernacular theology, or the relation between Porete and scholastic debates. Those are indispensable. But French, Spanish, and German sources bring out nuances that matter for philosophy of mind.

French work on the Mirror has emphasized its polyphonic form and its distinction between rational-discursive understanding and the understanding of love. This supports reading the book not as a linear treatise but as a staged conflict among mental powers.

Recent French scholarship on virtue and annihilation highlights an important issue: annihilation may be induced not simply by ascetic practice but by an intellectual apprehension of God’s greatness and the soul’s smallness. That matters because it prevents us from reducing Porete’s thought to moral discipline. Transformation is cognitive, affective, and volitional all at once.

Spanish scholarship has emphasized that Porete differs from some other women mystics by being less centered on erotic or bodily Christological imagery and more centered on divinity, Reason, Love, and negative knowledge. It also explicitly reads Love as a form of knowledge, opposing the understanding of Love to the understanding of Reason.

Latin American discussion has stressed the “more” beyond human understanding: knowledge as unknowing, being as “being without being,” and the soul’s entry into a pure “nothing” beyond ordinary thought.

German interpretation has pressed the political and institutional dimension of Reason, reading Porete’s Reason as a domesticated, disciplinary, juridical intellect. But the strongest version of that reading still insists that Porete is not abolishing thought; she is dramatizing another form of thinking, a thinking submerged in love.

French work on Porete’s metaphors, especially maritime and liquid imagery such as swimming, flowing, drops, and absorption, also matters philosophically. These images are not merely decorative; they give poetic form to a theory of mental transition from bounded selfhood into divine will.

13. A reconstructed Poretean philosophy of mind

Putting the pieces together, Porete’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as follows.

The mind is a created soul whose powers include memory, understanding, will, reason, love, sensation, and conscience. These powers are real, but their ordinary operation is marked by self-appropriation. The mind’s basic disorder is not merely ignorance or vice; it is proprietary self-relation.

Reason is necessary but limited. It can distinguish, question, teach, and guide, but it cannot grasp divine reality in the highest mode. When reason becomes sovereign, it becomes a barrier to the very truth it seeks.

Love is cognitive. The highest knowing is not detached representation but affective participation. Love generates divine knowing and transforms the knower.

The will is decisive. Proper will binds the soul to itself. The free return of will to God liberates the soul from self-possession. “No-will” is not psychological paralysis; it is the non-proprietary operation of will in union with divine will.

The self is not destroyed but de-owned. The soul becomes “without itself” when it no longer claims its own acts as possessions. Its highest agency is action in which God works in it, for it, and without its self-appropriating mediation.

Knowledge culminates in unknowing. The soul knows God, itself, and all things by being drawn into a divine knowing that exceeds objectification. The mind’s highest act is not conceptual mastery but humble participation.

Embodiment remains. The senses and bodily inclinations are not eliminated, but their ordinary usage can be suspended or transformed. Sin requires consent, so Porete distinguishes involuntary inclination from moral authorship.

God’s mind is archetypal. Divine knowing, willing, and loving are not external themes; they define what perfected mental life means. The soul’s mind is fulfilled when God sees, knows, loves, and acts in it.

Angelic minds are boundary cases. The Seraphim model immediate love without intermediary; guardian angels model action without encumbrance; Lucifer models the catastrophe of will that seeks by nature what must be received by grace.

Virtue and law are pedagogically necessary but not ultimate as external regimes. The annihilated soul is “above law” only because it is not “against law”: it is governed inwardly by divine love rather than outwardly by self-managed virtue.

14. Main cautions for interpretation

The first caution is that Porete’s “annihilation” should not be psychologized into depression, dissociation, or mere ego-loss. It is a theological-metaphysical transformation of will, cognition, and self-relation.

The second caution is that she should not be flattened into antinomianism. Her language about leaving the virtues is deliberately dangerous, and it was historically treated as dangerous, but the better reading is that she distinguishes lower virtue-governed agency from higher love-governed freedom.

The third caution is that she is not anti-reason in a simplistic way. She attacks the sovereignty of discursive reason, not all thought. Her text itself is highly intellectual, dialectical, and formally complex.

The fourth caution is that divine and angelic minds must not be dismissed as “theological background.” They are constitutive of her model. God is the archetype and final operator of mind; angels show created intelligence in immediate love, non-encumbered action, and fallen miswill.

The fifth caution is that her philosophy of mind is inseparable from literary form. The dialogue among Love, Reason, Soul, and the Virtues is itself a map of the soul’s internal powers. The Mirror does not merely state a theory of mind; it performs the conversion of mind from Reason’s jurisdiction to Love’s understanding.

Conclusion

Marguerite Porete’s thought yields a powerful and unusual philosophy of mind. It is not centered on representation, autonomy, or self-conscious mastery. It is centered on the transformation of the soul from self-possessing agency into non-proprietary participation in divine knowing, willing, and loving.

Her “mind” is the soul as a dynamic field of powers: reason, love, will, memory, understanding, sensation, conscience, and attention. These powers are not abolished at the summit; they are reordered. Reason loses sovereignty, love becomes intellective, will becomes free by ceasing to be proper will, and the self becomes most truly itself by being “without itself.”

The divine mind and angelic minds are indispensable to this system. God’s knowing and willing are the archetype and final mode of cognition; seraphic and angelic life reveal immediacy, hierarchy, and the stakes of will. Lucifer’s fall shows that even the highest created intelligence is ruined by possessive willing. The free soul’s union with God shows the opposite: intelligence perfected by dispossession.

A comprehensive Poretean philosophy of mind would therefore be a philosophy of de-owned consciousness: a theory in which the mind is fulfilled not by securing itself, but by surrendering self-ownership into a higher, loving, divine operation.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
